did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (custom CNN, ResNet-50, VGG-16) and classical machine learning (k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree) for multiclass dermatology image recognition across ten skin disease classes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT). It describes the development and evaluation of a custom CNN and comparison with other models (ResNet-50, VGG-16, k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree) on a curated dataset of 17,800 images, reporting specific metrics like 98% accuracy and macro F1 of 0.98. Although it contains a related work section, its primary purpose is reporting new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Comprehensive Analysis of Multiclass Dermatology Image Recognition Using Deep and Classical Machine Learning
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the need for accessible dermatology screening by developing a multiclass recognition system for ten skin conditions, including nine diseases and healthy skin. The authors curated a balanced dataset of 17,800 clinical images and evaluated a custom lightweight CNN against pretrained deep networks (ResNet-50, VGG-16) and classical machine learning models (k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree). Using a strict holdout protocol with 10% of data reserved for independent testing, the custom CNN achieved 98% accuracy and a macro F1 score of 0.98. The model significantly outperformed classical baselines and matched or exceeded the performance of the pretrained transfer learning models. Confusion analysis revealed that residual errors were primarily concentrated in illumination-sensitive pigmentary presentations. The results demonstrate that a compact, efficient custom CNN can provide reliable, calibrated predictions suitable for tele-dermatology and primary care triage in resource-constrained settings.

all_task_types.target: Ten discrete dermatological classes (nine disease categories and one healthy skin class)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin disease images into 10 categories using a custom CNN, ResNet-50, VGG-16, k-NN, SVM, and Decision Tree. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels with reported metrics including accuracy (98%), macro F1 (0.98), precision, recall, and confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: Curated Multiclass Dermatology Dataset
datasets_used.description: A balanced corpus of 17,800 clinical photographs spanning ten classes (nine disease categories and one healthy skin class). Images were drawn from varied sources to capture differences in skin tone, lighting, viewpoint, and capture device. The paper explicitly states all images are from publicly available sources, including a cited Mendeley dataset, with no external or private images added.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN, fine-tuning ResNet-50 and VGG-16, and training classical ML models (k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree) on fixed deep features.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and guiding tuning without leakage.
use.testing: Used for final independent evaluation of accuracy, precision, recall, F1, confusion matrices, and calibration. Reserved 10% of the data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To reserve 10% of the data for independent testing and partition the remaining 90% into training and validation sets for tuning without leakage.
To measure the proportion of correct predictions on the independent test set.
To evaluate the reliability of positive predictions for each class.
To evaluate the ability to identify all relevant instances within each class.
To provide a harmonic mean of precision and recall for each class.
To provide an unweighted mean of F1 scores across all classes, assessing overall multiclass performance.
To analyze residual errors and cross-class confusions, particularly for illumination-sensitive pigmentary presentations.
To assess the reliability of predicted probability estimates across classes.
To support threshold selection for referral and follow-up in triage.
To optimize the CNNs during training.
To guide the partitioning of feature space in Decision Tree models.
To classify fixed deep features extracted from the custom CNN.
To classify fixed deep features based on distance in feature space.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on point estimates of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores on an independent test set.

Curated Multiclass Dermatology Dataset.notes: The custom CNN achieved the highest overall accuracy (98%) and macro F1 (0.98). ResNet-50 and VGG-16 both achieved 97% accuracy. Classical models (k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree) performed lower, with accuracies ranging from 89% to 93%. Residual errors for deep models were concentrated in Light-Induced Pigment Disorders due to illumination sensitivity. Calibration analysis indicated reliable probability estimates across classes for the custom CNN.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom lightweight CNN, ResNet-50, VGG-16, k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 and VGG-16 were pretrained backbones fine-tuned on the dataset; custom CNN trained from scratch; classical models trained on fixed deep features from the custom CNN.

custom_cnn.description: Designed for accuracy and speed on clinical photos

resnet_50.structure: Residual blocks learning mapping F(x) added to input (y = x + F(x))

vgg_16.description: Straightforward CNN serving as a strong transfer baseline

k_nn.input: Fixed deep features from penultimate layer of custom CNN

svm.input: Fixed deep features from penultimate layer of custom CNN

decision_tree.input: Fixed deep features from penultimate layer of custom CNN

training_details.optimizer_details: Not explicitly named, but low-rate updates used for fine-tuning earlier blocks of pretrained models
training_details.tuning_method: Validation set guided hyperparameter tuning and early stopping
training_details.data_split: 90% development (80% train, 20% validation), 10% independent test set
training_details.augmentation: Resizing to 300x300, per-channel normalization, random rotations, flips, translations, zoom, brightness and contrast jitter

classification_or_output_head.custom_cnn: Not explicitly detailed beyond final classification layer implied by accuracy metrics
classification_or_output_head.resnet_50: Global average pooling feeding a task-specific classifier
classification_or_output_head.vgg_16: Global average pooling feeding a task-specific classifier
classification_or_output_head.classical_models: Class labels predicted via k-NN voting, SVM decision function, or Tree partitioning

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotations, flips, translations, and zoom applied to images during training.
Gentle brightness and contrast jitter applied to images during training.
All images resized to 300 x 300 pixels.
Per-channel normalization applied to standardize inputs.

conclusions: A custom lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN) achieved 98% accuracy and a macro F1 score of 0.98 on an independent test set of 1,780 images, outperforming classical machine learning baselines (k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree) and matching or exceeding the performance of pretrained deep networks (ResNet-50, VGG-16).
The custom CNN significantly outperformed classical machine learning models, which achieved lower accuracies ranging from 89% to 93%.

Residual classification errors were primarily concentrated in illumination-sensitive pigmentary presentations, specifically Light-Induced Pigment Disorders, indicating sensitivity to lighting conditions and texture.
Probability calibration analysis confirmed that the custom CNN produced reliable and well-aligned probability estimates across all ten classes, supporting its use for triage decision thresholds.

The model is compact and computationally efficient, making it suitable for integration into tele-dermatology and primary care triage workflows in resource-constrained settings where specialist access is limited.

The study demonstrates that a purpose-built, lightweight CNN can provide scalable and trustworthy screening support for multiclass dermatology image recognition when evaluated with rigorous holdout protocols and balanced datasets.
important_remarks: The dataset is curated from publicly available sources (including a Mendeley dataset) and is balanced across 10 classes, but lacks explicit external validation on independent clinical cohorts or diverse skin tones, limiting generalizability.

Residual classification errors are concentrated in illumination-sensitive pigmentary presentations, specifically Light-Induced Pigment Disorders, indicating sensitivity to lighting conditions and texture variations.
The study does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons rely on point estimates of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores.

Classical machine learning models (k-NN, SVM, Decision Tree) were trained on fixed deep features from the custom CNN rather than raw images, which may limit their independent feature learning capability compared to end-to-end deep learning.
The Decision Tree model showed a notably low recall (0.71) for the Healthy Skin class, reflecting susceptibility to background artifacts and over-splitting.
The k-NN model was highly sensitive to background texture and illumination, yielding the lowest recall (0.77) for Light-Induced Pigment Disorders among classical models.
While the paper emphasizes calibration and decision curves for clinical utility, it does not provide prospective trial data or clinician-in-the-loop evaluation results.

The model's compact size and high accuracy (98%) suggest potential for deployment in resource-constrained settings, but real-world performance may vary due to device heterogeneity and image variability not fully captured in the curated dataset.
